title: Feast and famine journal prompts
subtitle: A six-prompt set for the money pattern of big inflows followed by long dry months.
excerpt: A journal prompt set on the feast-and-famine money cycle. Two warm-up prompts to orient, then four narrower questions that walk the pattern down to where it lives. About thirty minutes, five minutes per prompt against a timer.
sourceNotes: Theme adapted from Day 4 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025). Free-write style from The Money Journal (Rebecca J Page, 2025). The six-prompt walk (general → specific) is original to the homemade.education library.

content.text: A six-prompt set on the feast-and-famine money cycle: the pattern where a big inflow arrives, then disappears, and the next one doesn't have a date. Six questions. Two warm-up, four narrower. About thirty minutes total, five minutes per prompt against a timer.
New to this style of journal practice? Read
journal prompts as practice
first.
The prompts
Warm-up
1. What does money feel like to me right now?
Just notice. The feeling in the body when you think about your accounts today. No need to fix it or interpret it. Write whatever comes.
2. Where am I in the cycle right now?
Just-after a big inflow? In the middle of a dry month? Waiting for something to land? Write where the money sits this week.
Closer in
3. The last time the money came in, what did I do with it?
Be specific. The actual transactions, in order, with what feeling. Don't soften the relief-spends or the panic-spends.
4. What did I expect to feel when the money arrived, and what did I actually feel?
The gap between the expected feeling and the actual one is often where the cycle holds. Write the gap.
5. When did the cycle start? Can I trace it back?
Some people remember the year, the job, the relationship. Others remember a parent who lived this way before them. Write whatever you can find.
6. Who in my life would have called a steady, large, sitting bank balance a problem?
Not who would have envied it. Who would have called it a problem. Write the names. Write the lines they used.
After the prompts

Close the notebook. Take one slow breath. You don't have to do anything with what you wrote. The act of writing it down is the practice; the insight tends to arrive over the next few days rather than at the end of the page.
If a line in your writing made you emotional, that's the line to come back to next week. Open the notebook to that page and write for five more minutes on it.
How it adapts

Other cycles, same shape: creative-work cycles, paid-leave cycles, commission cycles, royalty cycles. Substitute "the big inflow" for whatever the lump arrival is in your work, and the warm-up plus four narrower prompts still walk the pattern down.
Shorter version: just prompts 1, 3, and 6. Fifteen minutes total. Use when the full set is too much for the day but the pattern is still loud.

Spoken version: if you can't write, driving, walking, in the bath, speak the prompts aloud to a voice memo and answer the same way. The free-write principle still applies: don't edit, don't re-listen during the session.
Couple's version: each person writes their own answers in separate notebooks, then exchanges only what they want to share. Don't read each other's pages; the writing is for the writer.
Where this practice comes from

Theme adapted from Day 4 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025). The free-write method comes from across The Money Journal (Rebecca J Page, 2025), which holds 84 daily prompts. The general-to-specific six-prompt walk is original to the homemade.education library.
